name: Math
description: Math fab Mathonwy, a prominent figure in Welsh mythology and specifically within the Fourth Branch of the Mabinogi, stands as a king of Gwynedd, a region in North Wales. He is a complex and multifaceted character, often depicted as a powerful sorcerer and a wise ruler. His significance lies not only in his political authority but also in his deep connection to the magical and mystical aspects of the Welsh landscape. Math's reign is characterized by periods of peace and prosperity, yet it is frequently challenged by internal strife and external threats. He is a key player in many of the tales within the Mabinogion, often intervening directly to shape the destinies of other characters. His magical prowess is considerable, allowing him to manipulate reality and enforce his will. Math's legacy extends beyond his immediate actions, influencing the cultural and spiritual landscape of early Welsh society. He is a figure of considerable importance for understanding the complexities of power, magic, and social order in medieval Welsh literature.
subtitle: King and Sorcerer of Gwynedd

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Lleu Llaw Gyffes
keyMyths.description: Math's need to keep his feet in the lap of a virgin when not at war sets in motion the events leading to the birth of Lleu Llaw Gyffes. Gilfaethwy, Math's nephew, becomes infatuated with Goewin, the virgin foot-holder. Gwydion, Gilfaethwy's brother, orchestrates a war to draw Math away, allowing Gilfaethwy to violate Goewin. Upon Math's return, he punishes Gwydion and Gilfaethwy by transforming them into a series of mating pairs of animals. To compensate Goewin, Math marries her, restoring her honor. Math then uses his magic to impregnate Goewin with two twin sons, setting the stage for Lleu's birth.

keyMyths.title: The Naming of Lleu
keyMyths.description: Lleu Llaw Gyffes's mother, Arianrhod, refuses to acknowledge him as her son. Gwydion, however, tricks Arianrhod into naming and arming Lleu, thus fulfilling the necessary rites of passage. Arianrhod places three curses (or 'geasa') upon Lleu: that he should never have a name unless given by her, that he should never bear arms unless she herself equipped him, and that he should never have a wife from any race that dwells on Earth. Gwydion circumvents these curses through trickery and magic, highlighting Math's role as a facilitator of these events through his magical knowledge and support of Gwydion.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Blodeuwedd
keyMyths.description: To fulfill the third curse placed upon Lleu, Gwydion and Math create a wife for him out of flowers: meadowsweet, broom, and oak. They name her Blodeuwedd, meaning 'flower face.' However, Blodeuwedd proves to be unfaithful, conspiring with Gronw Pebr to murder Lleu. This act of betrayal demonstrates the inherent dangers of magic and the unintended consequences of manipulating nature. When Lleu is nearly killed, Gwydion nursed him back to health and punished Blodeuwedd by turning her into an owl.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Scandinavian and Celtic Religions
sources.description: Explores the symbolic significance of various elements within Celtic mythology, providing a deeper understanding of the meaning behind Math's actions and attributes.
